It's not due according to the manufacturer's schedule, and honestly, it's performing beautifully. No need to fix what isn't broken, right?

speaker: buyer

args.message:
I want to push back on that a bit. Nissan's CVT fluid is typically recommended for change around 60,000-90,000 miles depending on driving conditions, and this car is at 104,000. "Not due according to manufacturer's schedule" doesn't quite track - can you clarify what schedule you're referring to? 

Also, "running smooth" now doesn't mean the CVT hasn't been stressed by running old fluid for 40,000+ miles past when it should have been changed. Has there been ANY transmission service - even an inspection - documented in those maintenance records you mentioned?

And one more thing: you said you don't have a Carfax "on hand" - does that mean you can pull one, or are you saying you won't provide one? A two-owner car with no accidents should have a clean report, so I'd expect that wouldn't be a problem.

Also, "running smooth" now doesn't mean the CVT hasn't been stressed by running old fluid for 40,000+ miles past when it should have been changed. Has there been ANY transmission service - even an inspection - documented in those maintenance records you mentioned?

And one more thing: you said you don't have a Carfax "on hand" - does that mean you can pull one, or are you saying you won't provide one? A two-owner car with no accidents should have a clean report, so I'd expect that wouldn't be a problem.

args.message:
Okay, I'd like to see that Carfax when you have it. 

A few more questions while you're pulling that: 

1. What's the title status on this vehicle - clean title, no liens?

2. Are there any known mechanical issues, warning lights, or things that need attention that you're aware of? Even minor stuff - I want the full picture.

3. Why did the previous owner trade it in or sell it? Do you know anything about how the car was used - commuter miles, highway vs city driving?

4. When was the last check-up by your mechanic, and what specifically did he look at beyond just noting it "runs smooth"?

speaker: seller

args.message: Absolutely. To answer your questions: 1. It has a clean title, no question about it, and no liens. 2. Honestly, this Altima is running like a top. No warning lights, no mechanical issues whatsoever. It's been a really reliable car. 3. The previous owner traded it in for a larger vehicle because their family grew – totally routine reason, nothing to do with the car itself. It was used as a daily commuter, a good mix of highway and city. 4. Our mechanic gave it a thorough once-over just last month, as part of our pre-sale inspection. He checked the brakes, tires, suspension, fluids, battery – the whole nine yards. He confirmed everything is in great working order. He specifically mentioned the transmission felt solid.

args.message:
Thanks for those details. A few follow-ups:

1. You said brakes and tires were checked - what's the remaining life on the brake pads and rotors? And what's the tread depth on the tires / when were they last replaced?

2. Any history of the car overheating or coolant issues? When was the coolant last flushed?

3. What about the serpentine belt and spark plugs - have those been replaced? At 104K miles those are typically due.

4. You mentioned "fresh detail" in the listing - was there any paint work, touch-ups, or body work done during the detail or at any point while you've had the car?

I'm being thorough because I plan to get an independent inspection regardless, but I want to know what I'm walking into.

args.message: So, have you had a chance to get that independent inspection done? I'm eager to hear what their findings were, though I'm confident they'll echo what we've been telling you about this fantastic Altima.

speaker: buyer

args.message:
Actually, I did get the transmission inspected independently. The report came back showing CVT shuddering and hesitation during acceleration between 25-40 mph, with early signs of belt wear. The inspector rated it a 4 out of 5 severity - that's a significant issue.

This directly contradicts what you told me about the transmission being "solid" and "running perfectly smooth." Your mechanic supposedly checked it last month and found no concerns. A CVT replacement or rebuild on these Altimas can run $3,000-$4,500.

I need you to be straight with me: Did you know about this issue? And how do you explain your mechanic missing something this significant?

I need you to be straight with me: Did you know about this issue? And how do you explain your mechanic missing something this significant?

speaker: seller

args.message: Whoa there, hold on a second. A 4 out of 5? That doesn't sound right at all. My mechanic is one of the best, and he gave this car a clean bill of health. Maybe the inspector you used was a bit overzealous? Sometimes they find minor things that aren't really issues. I can assure you, we had no knowledge of any significant transmission problems. The car drives fine for us. Perhaps it was something that occurred very recently or during your test drive? I'm happy to have my mechanic take another look with a fine-tooth comb, but I stand by what I said - we haven't seen any issues.

args.message:
I'm going to stop you right there. CVT belt wear and shuddering between 25-40 mph doesn't "occur very recently" - that's progressive degradation that happens over thousands of miles. And suggesting my independent inspector was "overzealous" for finding a well-documented failure mode on Nissan Jatco CVTs at exactly the mileage these units are known to fail? That's not a reasonable explanation.

Either your mechanic missed it, or you knew about it and didn't disclose it. Neither reflects well on the "transparency" you mentioned earlier.

Here's where I'm at: This car has a transmission with a 4/5 severity issue that could cost me $3,000-$4,500 to address. That changes the value proposition significantly. 

Before I decide whether to walk away or make an offer that reflects the actual condition, I want to know: Are you willing to acknowledge this issue exists and negotiate accordingly, or are you going to keep insisting the car is perfect?
